comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around allegations of fake compliance services, particularly focusing on Delve's practices, the legitimacy of certifications, and the broader implications for startups and regulatory standards. Participants express skepticism, critique industry norms, and debate the ethical and practical aspects of compliance automation.

comments.author: OsrsNeedsf2P
comments.text:
Even if this is a hit piece made by a competitor, the evidence put forwards is very damning:

> Conclusions present before customer signs or provides info

If false, the defamation damages here would be in the tens of millions. Huge respect to whoever stuck their neck out to post this.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the seriousness of the allegations, emphasizing the potential for significant damages if proven true, and demonstrates a cautious, analytical perspective.

comments.author: suriya-ganesh
comments.text:
I've gone through this process and is this not a failure from the institute that are giving away these certifications for a fee without any due diligence?

intermediaries like delve have only amplified this failure.

it was obvious to anyone who was involved in this industry that, all of this is just security theatre with nothing really to back it up.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a critical point about systemic flaws in certification processes, questioning the integrity of the issuing bodies and the potential for fraud.

comments.author: cwal37
comments.text:
Delve seems clearly scummy, but dear god the author's company was also engaging in fraud with their own customers and just hoping to skate by.

"The trouble starts when you look at the answers Delve’s AI provided. Based on what your Delve policies claim, the questionnaire AI answers questions stating you have an MDM, had a 200 hour pen-test performed, and do regular backup restoration simulations. Tens of questions are answered like that. Great, you just lied to your vendor but at least you have a good shot at landing the deal. So what did we do? We kept our mouths shut."

Pretty rotten stuff. I went from energy into the software startup world and as I've gotten further down that road and energy has become more and more of a hot field I've encountered a depressing increase in that "just do it to make a deal" ethos, but in critical infrastructure.

Like, no, former Apple PM who learned about an interconnection queue from ChatGPT last week, you are not going to fix the grid, and even moreso you can't "just do X and ask forgiveness later", not in electricity.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a nuanced critique, acknowledging Delve's misconduct while also pointing out issues within the author's company, suggesting industry-wide problems.

comments.author: latchkey
comments.text:
I've been talking about this for a while now. For those of you thinking... Oh, I use a "good" company... think otherwise.

https://x.com/HotAisle/status/1946302651383329081

The whole thing is a racket.
comments.analysis: This comment warns users to be skeptical of compliance claims and suggests that many companies claiming to be compliant may be engaging in similar misconduct.

comments.author: debarshri
comments.text: This could be AI's globe.com moment
comments.analysis: The comment speculates on the potential for AI to disrupt or expose issues in the compliance industry, implying a significant impact akin to historic moments.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text:
Notice how none of Delve's affiliates on X are posting anything after that Substack post. Probably their lawyers told them not to say anything further.

What does that tell you about the scam that was unveiled?

Not good.
comments.analysis: This observation suggests a strategic silence from Delve's affiliates, hinting at legal concerns and the seriousness of the allegations.

comments.author: stuckkeys
comments.text: Great write up. What makes this interesting...I thought it was cool what they were doing...but also seemed too good to be true. I went ahead a booked a demo call with them. Great personas. Very friendly. Can't say they had all the answers, but they did bring a CISO on the last meeting, which seemed a bit scripted. They also never disclosed any breaches, even after I asked them. Yikes. Good luck to the orgs that went through all that process.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects skepticism about the industry's claims, illustrating a cautious approach and personal experience with the company's perceived professionalism.
